questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy in a 60 year old male with severe pancytopenia shows a marrow cellularity of 10% with the remainder being fat cells. There is no evidence of fibrosis or abnormal cells. What is the most likely mechanism for his anemia?
questions.explanation: The description (hypocellular marrow replaced by fat) defines Aplastic Anemia, which is most commonly caused by T-cell mediated suppression of stem cells. Option A describes Myelophthisic anemia. Option B describes Megaloblastic anemia. Option D is iron deficiency. Option E is hemolysis.

options.A: Replacement of marrow by malignant cells
options.B: Ineffective erythropoiesis due to DNA synthesis defect
options.C: Immune-mediated suppression of hematopoietic stem cells
options.E: Accelerated destruction of RBCs in the reticuloendothelial system

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group A positive is accidentally transfused with B positive blood. Which of the following initial biochemical changes is most likely to occur in this patient's blood?
questions.explanation: In acute intravascular hemolysis, free hemoglobin is released into the plasma and binds to haptoglobin; thus, haptoglobin levels drop significantly. Option A is incorrect because haptoglobin is consumed. Option B is incorrect because unconjugated bilirubin increases. Option C is incorrect because serum free hemoglobin increases. Option E is incorrect as fibrinogen may decrease in associated DIC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following specific hematological findings differentiates Beta-Thalassemia Trait from Iron Deficiency Anemia in a patient with a microcytic blood picture?
questions.explanation: HbA2 > 3.5% is a diagnostic hallmark of Beta-Thalassemia trait. Option A, B, and E are common to both. Option D (increased RDW) is more characteristic of Iron Deficiency Anemia, while Thalassemia trait usually has a normal (narrow) RDW.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with pallor and fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this microcytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia where the MCV is typically below 80 fL. Distractor A and B are incorrect because B12 and Folate deficiencies cause macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL). Distractor D is incorrect as aplastic anemia is usually normocytic. Distractor E is incorrect as alcoholism typically leads to macrocytosis.

options.A: Vitamin B12 deficiency [B]: Folate deficiency [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Aplastic anemia [E]: Chronic alcoholism
options.B: Folate deficiency [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Aplastic anemia [E]: Chronic alcoholism
options.C: Iron deficiency [D]: Aplastic anemia [E]: Chronic alcoholism

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production of neutrophils in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (Filgrastim) specifically stimulates the proliferation and differentiation of neutrophil progenitors. Distractor A is incorrect because Erythropoietin stimulates RBC production. Distractor C is incorrect as Thrombopoietin stimulates platelet production. Distractor D is incorrect as IL-5 is specific for eosinophils. Distractor E is incorrect as IL-11 stimulates megakaryocytes.

options.A: Erythropoietin [B]: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) [C]: Thrombopoietin [D]: Interleukin-5 [E]: Interleukin-11
options.B: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) [C]: Thrombopoietin [D]: Interleukin-5 [E]: Interleukin-11

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old patient with Blood Group A negative requires an urgent blood transfusion. Which of the following blood groups can be safely administered to this patient?
questions.explanation: O negative is the universal donor and lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, making it safe for an A negative patient. Distractor A is incorrect because Rh positive blood can cause sensitization in an Rh negative patient. Distractor B and C are incorrect because the patient has anti-B antibodies. Distractor E is incorrect because the Rh antigen (positive) is present.

options.A: A positive [B]: B negative [C]: AB negative [D]: O negative [E]: O positive
options.B: B negative [C]: AB negative [D]: O negative [E]: O positive
options.C: AB negative [D]: O negative [E]: O positive

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a routine blood examination, a patient is found to have a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 with 80 percent neutrophils. This reactive neutrophilic leukocytosis is most commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections typically trigger a release of neutrophils from the bone marrow, leading to neutrophilia. Distractor A is incorrect as viruses usually cause lymphocytosis. Distractor C and D are incorrect as they typically cause eosinophilia. Distractor E is incorrect as it is a malignant proliferation, not a reactive process.

options.A: Viral infections [B]: Acute bacterial infections [C]: Parasitic infestations [D]: Allergic reactions [E]: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
options.B: Acute bacterial infections [C]: Parasitic infestations [D]: Allergic reactions [E]: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
options.C: Parasitic infestations [D]: Allergic reactions [E]: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory parameter is the most sensitive indicator of early iron deficiency before anemia actually develops?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the first lab value to decrease in iron deficiency. Distractors A, C, and D are incorrect because hemoglobin, MCH, and hematocrit only decrease once the deficiency has progressed to overt anemia. Distractor E is incorrect as reticulocyte count is usually low or normal in iron deficiency and is not a specific diagnostic tool for it.

options.A: Hemoglobin levels [B]: Serum Ferritin [C]: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) [D]: Hematocrit [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.B: Serum Ferritin [C]: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) [D]: Hematocrit [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.C: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) [D]: Hematocrit [E]: Reticulocyte count

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Megaloblastic anemia is characterized by which of the following findings on a peripheral blood smear?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia alongside macro-ovalocytes. Distractor A is seen in iron deficiency. Distractor C is seen in Thalassemia or Liver disease. Distractor D indicates microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Distractor E is characteristic of Hereditary Spherocytosis.

options.A: Microcytic hypochromic cells [B]: Hypersegmented neutrophils [C]: Target cells [D]: Schistocytes [E]: Spherocytes
options.B: Hypersegmented neutrophils [C]: Target cells [D]: Schistocytes [E]: Spherocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with spontaneous skin petechiae and mucosal bleeding. The platelet count is 20,000/mm3. This condition is best classified as a disorder of:
questions.explanation: Primary hemostasis involves platelets and the vessel wall; thrombocytopenia (low platelets) is a classic primary hemostatic defect. Distractors A and B refer to coagulation factor deficiencies which typically present with deep tissue hematomas, not petechiae. Distractor D and E are less common causes of this specific presentation compared to low platelets.

options.A: Secondary hemostasis [B]: Coagulation factors [C]: Primary hemostasis [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Vascular wall integrity
options.B: Coagulation factors [C]: Primary hemostasis [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Vascular wall integrity
options.C: Primary hemostasis [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Vascular wall integrity

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is a classic clinical feature of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction due to ABO incompatibility?
questions.explanation: Acute intravascular hemolysis leads to the release of free hemoglobin into the blood, which is then excreted in urine (hemoglobinuria). Distractor A is incorrect because jaundice in acute reactions is not delayed. Distractor C is a feature of allergic reactions, not necessarily hemolysis. Distractor D is a complication of chronic transfusions. Distractor E is an increase in RBC mass, the opposite of hemolysis.

options.A: Delayed jaundice [B]: Hemoglobinuria [C]: Skin rashes [D]: Iron overload [E]: Polycythemia
options.B: Hemoglobinuria [C]: Skin rashes [D]: Iron overload [E]: Polycythemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemias, a Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) that is significantly low indicates that the cells are:
questions.explanation: MCHC measures the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells; low MCHC means the cells are pale or hypochromic. Distractor A refers to size (MCV). Distractor B refers to high MCHC (rarely seen except in spherocytosis). Distractor C refers to normal size. Distractor E refers to a specific type of macrocytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which malignant disorder of white blood cells is characterized by the presence of the Philadelphia chromosome (t9;22)?
questions.explanation: CML is specifically associated with the translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22, creating the BCR-ABL fusion gene. Distractors A, C, D, and E have different genetic associations or are classified differently (e.g., lymphomas).

options.A: Acute Myeloid Leukemia [B]: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia [C]: Hodgkin Lymphoma [D]: Multiple Myeloma [E]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
options.B: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia [C]: Hodgkin Lymphoma [D]: Multiple Myeloma [E]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
options.C: Hodgkin Lymphoma [D]: Multiple Myeloma [E]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with chronic kidney disease presents with worsening anemia. He is prescribed recombinant Erythropoietin. What is the primary mechanism of action of this drug?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin acts on erythroid progenitors (CFU-E) in the bone marrow to stimulate their proliferation and differentiation into proerythroblasts. Distractor A is the role of hepcidin regulation/vitamin C. Distractor C is incorrect as EPO does not affect RBC lifespan. Distractor D is physiologically counterproductive. Distractor E is related to B12 absorption.

options.A: Increases iron absorption from the gut [B]: Stimulates the differentiation of proerythroblasts [C]: Inhibits the destruction of old red blood cells [D]: Decreases the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin [E]: Promotes the production of intrinsic factor
options.B: Stimulates the differentiation of proerythroblasts [C]: Inhibits the destruction of old red blood cells [D]: Decreases the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin [E]: Promotes the production of intrinsic factor
options.C: Inhibits the destruction of old red blood cells [D]: Decreases the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin [E]: Promotes the production of intrinsic factor
options.D: Decreases the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin [E]: Promotes the production of intrinsic factor
options.E: Promotes the production of intrinsic factor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is suspected of having a bleeding disorder. Laboratory tests show a prolonged Bleeding Time, but a normal Platelet Count, normal Prothrombin Time (PT), and prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: vWD involves a deficiency of vWF which is needed for platelet adhesion (prolonging bleeding time) and carries Factor VIII (prolonging aPTT). Distractors A and B only prolong aPTT (normal bleeding time). Distractor D affects PT/aPTT but not bleeding time. Distractor E would show low platelets and prolonged PT/aPTT.

options.A: Hemophilia A [B]: Hemophilia B [C]: Von Willebrand Disease (vWD) [D]: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.B: Hemophilia B [C]: Von Willebrand Disease (vWD) [D]: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.C: Von Willebrand Disease (vWD) [D]: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.D: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: On a peripheral blood film of a 60-year-old patient, many Smudge cells are seen. The total leukocyte count is 40,000/mm3, with 90 percent mature-looking lymphocytes. This is most suggestive of:
questions.explanation: CLL classically presents with an absolute lymphocytosis of mature cells and fragile lymphocytes that rupture during slide preparation, forming smudge cells. Distractor A shows atypical (reactive) lymphocytes. Distractor B involves lymphoblasts (immature). Distractor D and E present more commonly with nodal disease or plasma cell abnormalities rather than high peripheral mature lymphocyte counts.

options.A: Infectious Mononucleosis [B]: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia [C]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) [D]: Follicular Lymphoma [E]: Multiple Myeloma
options.B: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia [C]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) [D]: Follicular Lymphoma [E]: Multiple Myeloma
options.C: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) [D]: Follicular Lymphoma [E]: Multiple Myeloma

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 4-year-old child presents with a microcytic anemia. The Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC count) is calculated to be 10. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?
questions.explanation: A Mentzer index less than 13 suggests Thalassemia, while a value greater than 13 suggests Iron deficiency. Distractor A is incorrect because the index is low. Distractors C, D, and E are causes of microcytic anemia but do not typically present with the high RBC count relative to MCV seen in Thalassemia trait.

options.A: Iron deficiency anemia [B]: Beta-Thalassemia trait [C]: Sideroblastic anemia [D]: Lead poisoning [E]: Anemia of chronic disease
options.B: Beta-Thalassemia trait [C]: Sideroblastic anemia [D]: Lead poisoning [E]: Anemia of chronic disease
options.C: Sideroblastic anemia [D]: Lead poisoning [E]: Anemia of chronic disease
options.D: Lead poisoning [E]: Anemia of chronic disease

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following immunologic mechanisms is responsible for the destruction of red blood cells in an Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction?
questions.explanation: ABO incompatibility causes pre-formed IgM antibodies to bind to donor RBCs, activating the classical complement pathway and causing intravascular hemolysis, which is a Type II hypersensitivity. Distractor A is allergic. Distractor C is serum sickness/SLE style. Distractor D is delayed/cellular. Distractor E is a different transfusion complication entirely.

options.A: Type I Hypersensitivity (IgE mediated) [B]: Type II Hypersensitivity (Complement mediated) [C]: Type III Hypersensitivity (Immune complex) [D]: Type IV Hypersensitivity (T-cell mediated) [E]: Graft versus Host Disease
options.B: Type II Hypersensitivity (Complement mediated) [C]: Type III Hypersensitivity (Immune complex) [D]: Type IV Hypersensitivity (T-cell mediated) [E]: Graft versus Host Disease
options.C: Type III Hypersensitivity (Immune complex) [D]: Type IV Hypersensitivity (T-cell mediated) [E]: Graft versus Host Disease
options.D: Type IV Hypersensitivity (T-cell mediated) [E]: Graft versus Host Disease

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old vegan male presents with paresthesias in his feet and a stumbling gait. Peripheral smear shows macro-ovalocytes and a bone marrow biopsy reveals a hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic erythropoiesis. What is the most likely biochemical defect?
questions.explanation: B12 and Folate are cofactors for DNA synthesis; their deficiency leads to nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony (megaloblastic changes). Distractor A is the defect in iron deficiency. Distractor C is not a recognized pathological mechanism for megaloblastic anemia. Distractor D causes spherocytosis. Distractor E causes oxidative hemolysis.

options.A: Impaired hemoglobin synthesis [B]: Impaired DNA synthesis [C]: Excessive RNA production [D]: Defective spectrin in RBC membrane [E]: Deficiency of G6PD enzyme
options.B: Impaired DNA synthesis [C]: Excessive RNA production [D]: Defective spectrin in RBC membrane [E]: Deficiency of G6PD enzyme
options.C: Excessive RNA production [D]: Defective spectrin in RBC membrane [E]: Deficiency of G6PD enzyme
options.D: Defective spectrin in RBC membrane [E]: Deficiency of G6PD enzyme

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with acute bleeding. Lab results: PT 25s (Ref 11-13s), aPTT 60s (Ref 25-35s), Fibrinogen 50 mg/dL (Ref 200-400), and D-dimer markedly elevated. This profile is diagnostic of:
questions.explanation: DIC is characterized by consumption of factors (prolonged PT/aPTT), consumption of fibrinogen (low levels), and secondary fibrinolysis (high D-dimers). Distractor A only prolongs aPTT. Distractor B can mimic this but usually doesn't have the same degree of D-dimer elevation. Distractors D and E affect specific pathways but do not typically consume fibrinogen to this level.

options.A: Hemophilia A [B]: Liver failure [C]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) [D]: Heparin overdose [E]: Warfarin toxicity
options.B: Liver failure [C]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) [D]: Heparin overdose [E]: Warfarin toxicity
options.C: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) [D]: Heparin overdose [E]: Warfarin toxicity

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with a suspected myelodysplastic syndrome, the presence of Ring Sideroblasts in the bone marrow indicates an abnormality in:
questions.explanation: Ring sideroblasts occur when iron accumulates in the mitochondria of erythroid precursors due to defective heme synthesis. Distractor A is Thalassemia. Distractor C is a general epigenetic mechanism. Distractor D and E are megaloblastic mechanisms.

options.A: Globin chain synthesis [B]: Mitochondrial iron utilization [C]: DNA methylation [D]: Vitamin B12 absorption [E]: Intracellular folate transport
options.B: Mitochondrial iron utilization [C]: DNA methylation [D]: Vitamin B12 absorption [E]: Intracellular folate transport
options.C: DNA methylation [D]: Vitamin B12 absorption [E]: Intracellular folate transport
options.D: Vitamin B12 absorption [E]: Intracellular folate transport

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female is found to have anemia, thrombocytopenia, and fever. Peripheral smear shows numerous schistocytes. Coagulation studies (PT and aPTT) are normal. What is the most likely pathophysiology?
questions.explanation: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP) presents with the pentad of fever, anemia (hemolytic with schistocytes), thrombocytopenia, renal failure, and neuro symptoms. Crucially, PT and aPTT are normal in TTP, distinguishing it from DIC. Distractor A (ITP) doesn't have schistocytes. Distractor C (DIC) has abnormal PT/aPTT. Distractor D and E do not fit this lab profile.

options.A: Autoimmune destruction of platelets [B]: Deficiency of ADAMTS13 enzyme [C]: Sepsis leading to DIC [D]: Snake venom poisoning [E]: Hypersplenism
options.B: Deficiency of ADAMTS13 enzyme [C]: Sepsis leading to DIC [D]: Snake venom poisoning [E]: Hypersplenism
options.C: Sepsis leading to DIC [D]: Snake venom poisoning [E]: Hypersplenism

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is receiving G-CSF for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. He complains of significant deep-seated bone pain. What is the most likely cause of this adverse effect?
questions.explanation: Bone pain is the most common side effect of G-CSF, caused by the rapid expansion of hematopoietic cells within the marrow cavity. Distractor A is a possibility in cancer patients but the timing with G-CSF makes B more likely. Distractor C and D are not standard side effects of G-CSF. Distractor E can happen due to cell turnover but is less common than simple marrow expansion pain.

options.A: Metastatic bone disease [B]: Bone marrow expansion [C]: Hypocalcemia [D]: Osteonecrosis [E]: Acute gouty arthritis
options.B: Bone marrow expansion [C]: Hypocalcemia [D]: Osteonecrosis [E]: Acute gouty arthritis

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old male presents with fatigue. His laboratory reports show a Hemoglobin of 10 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification of this anemia?
questions.explanation:
Logic: An MCV below 80 fL is defined as microcytic. Low hemoglobin (anemia) with small red cells is typically microcytic hypochromic. 
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a severe bacterial infection shows a White Blood Cell (WBC) count of 18,000/mm3 with an increase in immature neutrophils (band cells). This phenomenon is termed as:
questions.explanation:
Logic: A left shift refers to the release of immature precursors (bands, metamyelocytes) from the bone marrow during an active inflammatory or infectious response.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with Blood Group O has which of the following characteristics?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Group O individuals lack A and B antigens but possess the precursor H antigen and naturally occurring IgM antibodies against A and B.
Debunking:

options.A: Presence of A and B antigens on RBC surface
options.B: Absence of both Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies in serum
options.C: Presence of H antigen and both Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies
options.E: Presence of A antigen and Anti-B antibody

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear examination reveals the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (6 or more lobes). This finding is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation:
Logic: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic feature of megaloblastic anemia resulting from Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency, reflecting impaired DNA synthesis.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is considered a first-line laboratory investigation to evaluate the intrinsic pathway of coagulation?
questions.explanation:
Logic: aPTT measures the integrity of the intrinsic and common pathways of coagulation.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Anemia caused by the premature destruction of red blood cells, characterized by an increase in reticulocyte count, is classified as:
questions.explanation:
Logic: Hemolytic anemias involve shortened RBC lifespan and a compensatory erythroid hyperplasia in the marrow, leading to high reticulocyte counts.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following blood components is primarily affected in a patient with a diagnosis of Thrombocytopenia?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Thrombocytopenia is defined as a reduction in the platelet count (usually below 150,000/mm3).
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are primarily caused by:
questions.explanation:
Logic: Acute reactions usually occur due to ABO incompatibility where IgM antibodies bind to donor RBCs, activating the complement system and causing intravascular hemolysis.
Debunking:

options.B: Preformed IgM antibodies causing complement activation
options.E: Contamination of the blood bag with bacteria

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In which of the following conditions would you expect to see an increase in the number of Eosinophils on a differential WBC count?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Eosinophilia is characteristic of Type I hypersensitivity reactions (like asthma) and parasitic infestations.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with pica and brittle nails. Labs show Hgb 9 g/dL, MCV 70 fL, and Serum Ferritin 5 ng/mL. What is the most expected finding regarding her Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC)?
questions.explanation:
Logic: In iron deficiency anemia, serum iron and ferritin are low, but the body increases the production of transferrin (measured as TIBC) to maximize iron transport.
Debunking:

options.E: TIBC cannot be measured in anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy of a 50-year-old man shows hypocellularity with replacement of hematopoietic elements by fat cells. His peripheral blood shows a reduction in RBCs, WBCs, and platelets. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Pancytopenia (low RBC, WBC, Platelets) associated with a "dry" or "fatty" marrow is the hallmark of aplastic anemia.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the laboratory diagnosis of Megaloblastic Anemia, which of the following biochemical findings helps differentiate Vitamin B12 deficiency from Folate deficiency?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Both deficiencies cause high homocysteine, but only Vitamin B12 deficiency leads to an increase in Methylmalonic Acid because B12 is a required cofactor for the conversion of methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 4-year-old child of Mediterranean descent presents with severe anemia and frontal bossing. Peripheral smear shows numerous target cells and nucleated red blood cells. What is the underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation:
Logic: The clinical description (Mediterranean, skeletal changes, target cells) fits Beta-Thalassemia Major, which is caused by a defect in globin chain synthesis.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory findings is most characteristic of a Reactive Leukemoid Reaction, helping to distinguish it from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)?
questions.explanation:
Logic: A leukemoid reaction (reactive neutrophilia) shows high LAP scores (mature neutrophils are functional), whereas CML shows very low LAP scores (neoplastic neutrophils are enzymatically deficient).
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old patient treated with high-dose recombinant Erythropoietin for chronic kidney disease-related anemia develops a sudden increase in blood pressure and a thrombotic stroke. What is the most likely mechanism for this adverse effect?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Rapid increases in hemoglobin or very high target levels of Hgb with EPO use increase blood viscosity and carry a significant risk of hypertension and thromboembolic events.
Debunking:

options.A: Direct vasoconstriction by the EPO molecule
options.B: Increase in blood viscosity and rapid rise in hemoglobin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following hematopoietic growth factors acts as a "lineage-unspecific" cytokine, acting on pluripotent stem cells to stimulate multiple cell lines including RBCs, granulocytes, and megakaryocytes?
questions.explanation:
Logic: IL-3 (also called multi-CSF) is a multilineage growth factor that acts on early progenitor cells to stimulate various lineages.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (Rh-negative) is in her second pregnancy. Her first child was Rh-positive. She did not receive anti-D prophylaxis. If the current fetus is Rh-positive, the newborn is at risk of Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN). What is the primary isotype of the antibody involved in crossing the placenta?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Only IgG antibodies can cross the placenta via Fc receptors. The mother's anti-D antibodies (formed during the first pregnancy) are IgG and can enter the fetal circulation to cause hemolysis.
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with mucosal bleeding and petechiae. Laboratory investigations show a normal Platelet count, normal PT, and normal aPTT, but the Bleeding Time (BT) is significantly prolonged. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Normal counts and coagulation times (PT/aPTT) with prolonged BT indicate a qualitative platelet defect or a vessel wall defect. Bernard-Soulier is a defect in platelet adhesion (GP Ib-IX-V).
Debunking:

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the interpretation of bone marrow findings in Iron Deficiency Anemia, which of the following is considered the "gold standard" for assessing iron stores?
questions.explanation:
Logic: Prussian blue (Perls' stain) specifically stains iron (hemosiderin). Its absence in the bone marrow is the definitive diagnostic feature for depleted iron stores.
Debunking:

options.A: Ratio of Myeloid to Erythroid cells (M:E ratio)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old student presents for a routine check-up. Her blood report shows a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following morphological classifications best describes this anemia?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV of less than 80 fL. Option A is incorrect as normocytic anemia involves an MCV between 80-100 fL. Option B and D are incorrect because macrocytic and megaloblastic anemias involve an MCV greater than 100 fL. Option E is a specific type of macrocytic anemia caused by Vitamin B12 deficiency.

options.A: Normocytic anemia [B]: Macrocytic anemia [C]: Microcytic anemia [D]: Megaloblastic anemia [E]: Pernicious anemia
options.B: Macrocytic anemia [C]: Microcytic anemia [D]: Megaloblastic anemia [E]: Pernicious anemia
options.C: Microcytic anemia [D]: Megaloblastic anemia [E]: Pernicious anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a primary hematopoietic growth factor required for red blood cell production. In a healthy adult, which organ is the primary site of its synthesis?
questions.explanation: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is synthesized by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Option A is incorrect because the liver is the primary site only during the fetal period. Option B, D, and E are incorrect as they are lymphoid or hematopoietic tissues but do not synthesize erythropoietin.

options.A: Liver [B]: Spleen [C]: Kidney [D]: Bone marrow [E]: Thymus

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child with a known history of worm infestation presents with pallor. Peripheral blood smear shows small red cells with a wide area of central pallor. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is characterized by microcytic (small) and hypochromic (increased central pallor) red blood cells. Option A and E are incorrect as they lead to macrocytic cells. Option C is incorrect as it results in normocytic normochromic anemia with pancytopenia. Option D typically shows normocytic anemia with regenerative features like reticulocytosis.

options.A: Folate deficiency anemia [B]: Iron deficiency anemia [C]: Aplastic anemia [D]: Hemolytic anemia [E]: Vitamin B12 deficiency
options.B: Iron deficiency anemia [C]: Aplastic anemia [D]: Hemolytic anemia [E]: Vitamin B12 deficiency
options.C: Aplastic anemia [D]: Hemolytic anemia [E]: Vitamin B12 deficiency

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following leukocytes is typically increased in the peripheral blood in response to an acute pyogenic bacterial infection?
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia (increased neutrophils) is the classic response to acute bacterial infections. Option A is incorrect as lymphocytosis is typical of viral infections. Option B is seen in chronic infections like TB. Option C is associated with allergic reactions or parasitic infestations. Option E is rare and seen in myeloproliferative disorders.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group AB positive requires a packed red cell transfusion. Which of the following donor blood groups can be safely administered to this patient?
questions.explanation: Individuals with blood group AB are universal recipients because their plasma lacks anti-A and anti-B antibodies. Option A, B, C, and E are incorrect because while they are safe, the patient is not limited to them; all ABO groups are compatible in an emergency or routine setting for an AB recipient.

options.A: Only AB positive [B]: Only O negative [C]: Only A positive and B positive [D]: Any ABO blood group [E]: Only AB negative
options.B: Only O negative [C]: Only A positive and B positive [D]: Any ABO blood group [E]: Only AB negative
options.C: Only A positive and B positive [D]: Any ABO blood group [E]: Only AB negative
options.D: Any ABO blood group [E]: Only AB negative

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Megaloblastic anemia is characterized by a defect in DNA synthesis leading to impaired cell division. Deficiency of which of the following nutrients primarily causes this condition?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 and Folate are essential cofactors for DNA synthesis; their deficiency leads to megaloblastic changes. Option A causes microcytic anemia. Option B is related to scurvy. Option D is involved in the synthesis of clotting factors. Option E is an antioxidant and its deficiency rarely causes hemolytic anemia in neonates.

options.A: Iron [B]: Vitamin C [C]: Vitamin B12 [D]: Vitamin K [E]: Vitamin E
options.B: Vitamin C [C]: Vitamin B12 [D]: Vitamin K [E]: Vitamin E
options.C: Vitamin B12 [D]: Vitamin K [E]: Vitamin E

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the laboratory investigation of a bleeding patient, the Prothrombin Time (PT) is used to assess which of the following?
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic and common pathways of coagulation (Factors VII, X, V, II, and I). Option A is incorrect because the intrinsic pathway is measured by Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT). Option C, D, and E are tests for platelet number and function, not the coagulation cascade pathways.

options.A: Intrinsic pathway [B]: Extrinsic pathway [C]: Platelet count [D]: Bleeding time [E]: Platelet aggregation
options.B: Extrinsic pathway [C]: Platelet count [D]: Bleeding time [E]: Platelet aggregation
options.C: Platelet count [D]: Bleeding time [E]: Platelet aggregation

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with small, pin-point non-blanching red spots on her legs (petechiae). This clinical finding is most characteristic of which type of disorder?
questions.explanation: Petechiae and mucosal bleeding are hallmark signs of thrombocytopenia or platelet dysfunction. Option A, C, D, and E typically present with large bruises (ecchymoses) or deep tissue hematomas and hemarthrosis rather than petechiae.

options.A: Coagulation factor deficiency [B]: Platelet disorder [C]: Vitamin K deficiency [D]: Hemophilia A [E]: Severe liver disease
options.B: Platelet disorder [C]: Vitamin K deficiency [D]: Hemophilia A [E]: Severe liver disease
options.C: Vitamin K deficiency [D]: Hemophilia A [E]: Severe liver disease

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) is used clinically to treat which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: G-CSF stimulates the production of granulocytes (primarily neutrophils) and is used to treat neutropenia, especially after chemotherapy. Option A is treated with Erythropoietin. Option B is treated with Thrombopoietin. Option D requires clotting factor concentrates. Option E is an excess of RBCs and is not treated with growth factors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction?
questions.explanation: Most acute hemolytic reactions result from preformed IgM antibodies in the recipient reacting against donor ABO antigens. Option A causes septic shock. Option B usually causes delayed reactions or HDFN. Option D causes urticaria or anaphylaxis. Option E is a chronic complication of multiple transfusions.

options.A: Bacterial contamination of blood [B]: Rh incompatibility [C]: ABO incompatibility [D]: Allergic reaction to plasma proteins [E]: Iron overload
options.B: Rh incompatibility [C]: ABO incompatibility [D]: Allergic reaction to plasma proteins [E]: Iron overload
options.C: ABO incompatibility [D]: Allergic reaction to plasma proteins [E]: Iron overload
options.D: Allergic reaction to plasma proteins [E]: Iron overload

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old chronic alcoholic presents with fatigue. Lab studies show MCV of 110 fL and hypersegmented neutrophils on peripheral smear. He also complains of tingling and numbness in his feet. Which deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Both Folate and B12 deficiency cause megaloblastic anemia (high MCV, hypersegmented neutrophils), but only B12 deficiency causes neurological symptoms (subacute combined degeneration). Option A is incorrect as folate deficiency has no neurological findings. Option B and D cause microcytic anemia. Option E (Thiamine) deficiency causes Beriberi but not megaloblastic anemia.

options.A: Folic acid [B]: Iron [C]: Vitamin B12 [D]: Pyridoxine [E]: Thiamine

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient has a prolonged Bleeding Time and a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT), but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT). Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?
questions.explanation: von Willebrand Disease causes a defect in platelet adhesion (prolonging Bleeding Time) and a deficiency in Factor VIII (prolonging APTT). Option A and B prolong only APTT. Option D prolongs only PT. Option E prolongs Bleeding Time but usually does not affect APTT unless extremely severe.

options.A: Hemophilia A [B]: Hemophilia B [C]: von Willebrand Disease [D]: Factor VII deficiency [E]: Thrombocytopenia
options.B: Hemophilia B [C]: von Willebrand Disease [D]: Factor VII deficiency [E]: Thrombocytopenia
options.C: von Willebrand Disease [D]: Factor VII deficiency [E]: Thrombocytopenia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old male has a total leukocyte count of 50,000/microL with many mature neutrophils and metamyelocytes. To differentiate a leukemoid reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which test is most useful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in reactive leukocytosis (leukemoid reaction) and characteristically low in CML. Option A is for erythropoietic activity. Option B, D are for hemostasis. Option E is for detecting abnormal hemoglobins like HbS.

options.A: Reticulocyte count [B]: Bleeding time [C]: Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [D]: Prothrombin time [E]: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
options.B: Bleeding time [C]: Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [D]: Prothrombin time [E]: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
options.C: Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [D]: Prothrombin time [E]: Hemoglobin electrophoresis

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, the patient develops fever, chills, and lower back pain. Which of the following mechanisms is responsible for the rapid destruction of red cells?
questions.explanation: Acute ABO incompatibility leads to IgM binding and immediate activation of the complement cascade, causing intravascular lysis. Option A and C are more typical of extravascular hemolysis. Option D is seen in G6PD deficiency. Option E is a consequence of hemolysis, not the mechanism of destruction.

options.A: Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) [B]: Complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis [C]: Splenic sequestration by macrophages [D]: Formation of Heinz bodies [E]: Accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin
options.B: Complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis [C]: Splenic sequestration by macrophages [D]: Formation of Heinz bodies [E]: Accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin
options.C: Splenic sequestration by macrophages [D]: Formation of Heinz bodies [E]: Accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin
options.D: Formation of Heinz bodies [E]: Accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear shows "Target cells" and "Howell-Jolly bodies". These findings are most commonly associated with which of the following states?
questions.explanation: The spleen normally removes nuclear remnants (Howell-Jolly bodies) and excess membrane; after splenectomy, these remain in circulation. Target cells are also seen in thalassemias and liver disease. Option A, C, D, and E do not typically show Howell-Jolly bodies.

options.A: Acute viral infection [B]: Post-splenectomy state [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Megaloblastic anemia [E]: Bacterial sepsis
options.B: Post-splenectomy state [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Megaloblastic anemia [E]: Bacterial sepsis
options.C: Iron deficiency [D]: Megaloblastic anemia [E]: Bacterial sepsis

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with Rheumatoid Arthritis has a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL. Lab results show low serum iron, low Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC), and high serum ferritin. What is the mechanism of this anemia?
questions.explanation: Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD) is characterized by high hepcidin levels which trap iron inside macrophages and hepatocytes, leading to low serum iron but high ferritin (stored iron). Option A is iron deficiency anemia where TIBC would be high and Ferritin low. Option B is megaloblastic anemia. Option D is Thalassemia. Option E is Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia.

options.A: Chronic blood loss [B]: Impaired DNA synthesis [C]: Sequestration of iron in macrophages [D]: Defect in globin chain synthesis [E]: Autoimmune destruction of RBCs
options.B: Impaired DNA synthesis [C]: Sequestration of iron in macrophages [D]: Defect in globin chain synthesis [E]: Autoimmune destruction of RBCs
options.C: Sequestration of iron in macrophages [D]: Defect in globin chain synthesis [E]: Autoimmune destruction of RBCs
options.D: Defect in globin chain synthesis [E]: Autoimmune destruction of RBCs

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy in a patient with severe pancytopenia shows over 90 percent fat replacement and very few hematopoietic cells. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by a "dry tap" or hypocellular marrow replaced by fat. Option A and E would show marrow infiltration by malignant cells (hypercellular). Option B shows extensive fibrosis (collagen deposition). Option D shows a hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic erythroid precursors.

options.A: Metastatic cancer [B]: Myelofibrosis [C]: Aplastic anemia [D]: Vitamin B12 deficiency [E]: Acute leukemia
options.B: Myelofibrosis [C]: Aplastic anemia [D]: Vitamin B12 deficiency [E]: Acute leukemia
options.C: Aplastic anemia [D]: Vitamin B12 deficiency [E]: Acute leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12-year-old boy presents with hemarthrosis of the knee. Laboratory findings show a normal PT, normal Bleeding Time, and a significantly prolonged APTT. The APTT corrects when mixed with normal plasma. Which factor is most likely deficient?
questions.explanation: A prolonged APTT that corrects with mixing suggests a factor deficiency in the intrinsic pathway (VIII, IX, XI, or XII). Hemarthrosis in a male is classic for Hemophilia A (Factor VIII). Option A prolongs PT. Option C prolongs Bleeding Time. Option D (Prothrombin) prolongs both PT and APTT. Option E is a platelet component.

options.A: Factor VII [B]: Factor VIII [C]: von Willebrand Factor [D]: Factor II [E]: Platelet Factor 3
options.B: Factor VIII [C]: von Willebrand Factor [D]: Factor II [E]: Platelet Factor 3
options.C: von Willebrand Factor [D]: Factor II [E]: Platelet Factor 3

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following hematopoietic growth factors is primarily responsible for the expansion of hematopoietic stem cells and multi-lineage progenitor cells?
questions.explanation: Stem Cell Factor (SCF) acts on the most primitive hematopoietic cells. Option A is lineage-specific for RBCs. Option C is lineage-specific for neutrophils. Option D is specific for eosinophil production. Option E is primarily for megakaryocyte/platelet production.

options.A: Erythropoietin [B]: Stem Cell Factor (c-kit ligand) [C]: G-CSF [D]: Interleukin-5 [E]: Thrombopoietin
options.B: Stem Cell Factor (c-kit ligand) [C]: G-CSF [D]: Interleukin-5 [E]: Thrombopoietin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with known Cold Agglutinin Disease (IgM mediated) develops sudden jaundice and splenomegaly after exposure to cold weather. What is the primary site of red cell destruction in this condition?
questions.explanation: While IgM can fix complement, Cold Agglutinins often lead to C3b coating of RBCs, which are then recognized and removed by splenic macrophages (extravascular hemolysis). Option A is more typical of paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria or acute ABO reactions. Option C, D, and E are not primary sites for red cell destruction in this pathology.

options.A: Intravascular (within blood vessels) [B]: Extravascular (in the spleen/liver) [C]: Bone marrow [D]: Kidney tubules [E]: Lung capillaries
options.B: Extravascular (in the spleen/liver) [C]: Bone marrow [D]: Kidney tubules [E]: Lung capillaries
options.C: Bone marrow [D]: Kidney tubules [E]: Lung capillaries

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory results show a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most appropriate classification for her anemia?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV of less than 80 fL. Since the patient has an MCV of 72 fL, she falls into this category. Option A is incorrect as macrocytic anemia requires an MCV greater than 100 fL. Option B is incorrect as normocytic anemia involves an MCV between 80-100 fL. Option D is incorrect as megaloblastic anemia is a subtype of macrocytic anemia. Option E is a mechanistic classification, not a morphologic one based on MCV.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a critical hematopoietic growth factor. In a healthy adult, which organ is the primary site of its production?
questions.explanation: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Option A is incorrect because the liver is the primary site only during the fetal period. Option B is incorrect as the bone marrow is the target organ, not the production site. Option C and E are incorrect as neither the spleen nor thymus produces significant erythropoietin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old man presents with a suspected diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia. Which of the following laboratory investigations is considered the most sensitive and specific initial indicator of depleted total body iron stores?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels reflect the amount of stored iron in the liver and bone marrow, making it the best non-invasive test for iron stores. Option A is incorrect because serum iron fluctuates and does not accurately reflect stores. Option C is incorrect as TIBC increases in iron deficiency but is less specific than ferritin. Option D is used for hemoglobinopathies like Thalassemia. Option E measures bone marrow activity, not iron stores.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Reactive leukocytosis is a common finding in various conditions. Which of the following conditions is most characteristically associated with eosinophilia?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is most commonly associated with allergic reactions and parasitic infections (helminths). Option A is incorrect as bacterial infections typically cause neutrophilia. Option B is incorrect as viral infections often cause lymphocytosis. Option D is incorrect as tissue necrosis usually leads to neutrophilia. Option E is incorrect as aplastic anemia results in pancytopenia (low counts).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient requires a blood transfusion in an emergency where their blood group is unknown. Which of the following blood groups is considered the universal donor for Packed Red Blood Cells?
questions.explanation: O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, meaning it will not be attacked by most recipients' antibodies. Option A, B, and E are incorrect as they possess antigens (A, B, or Rh) that could trigger a reaction. Option C is incorrect because AB positive is the universal recipient, not the donor.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory test is used to specifically screen for the integrity of the extrinsic and common pathways of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) evaluates the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways. Option A is incorrect as bleeding time assesses platelet function. Option B is incorrect as aPTT evaluates the intrinsic and common pathways. Option D is an outdated test for platelet function. Option E is incorrect as D-dimer measures fibrinolysis and clot breakdown.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hematopoietic growth factors are used therapeutically to manage cytopenias. Which of the following factors is specifically used to stimulate the production of neutrophils?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (Filgrastim) specifically stimulates the proliferation and differentiation of neutrophil progenitors. Option A is for RBCs. Option B and D are for platelets. Option E (GM-CSF) stimulates multiple lineages (neutrophils, monocytes, and eosinophils) but G-CSF is more specific for neutrophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemias, what does the term hypochromic refer to?
questions.explanation: Hypochromic refers to a decrease in hemoglobin concentration, appearing as an increased central pallor on a peripheral smear. Option A is anisocytosis. Option B is poikilocytosis. Option D is reticulocytosis. Option E is erythropoiesis under stress or marrow replacement.

options.A: Variation in the size of red blood cells
options.B: Variation in the shape of red blood cells
options.C: Reduced concentration of hemoglobin in red blood cells
options.E: Presence of nucleated red blood cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of megaloblastic anemia worldwide?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia is primarily caused by deficiencies in Vitamin B12 or Folate, which are essential for DNA synthesis. Option A and D are incorrect as they lead to iron deficiency (microcytic). Option C describes Thalassemias or Sickle Cell. Option E leads to myelophthisic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following cells is the precursor to platelets?
questions.explanation: Platelets are formed by the cytoplasmic fragmentation of megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. Option A is an RBC precursor. Option B is a granulocyte precursor. Option D is a lymphocyte precursor. Option E is a monocyte precursor.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a history of chronic alcoholism presents with anemia and numbness in his feet. A peripheral blood smear reveals macro-ovalocytes and neutrophils with six or more nuclear lobes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Both B12 and Folate deficiencies cause megaloblastic anemia (macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils). However, only Vitamin B12 deficiency is associated with neurological symptoms (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord). Option A, D, and E typically present with microcytic or normocytic features and lack hypersegmented neutrophils. Option B is incorrect because folate deficiency does not cause neurological deficits.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12-year-old child of Mediterranean descent presents with mild anemia. The peripheral smear shows many target cells and microcytic, hypochromic RBCs. Mentzer index (MCV/RBC count) is less than 13. Which condition should be suspected?
questions.explanation: Thalassemia trait typically shows microcytosis out of proportion to the anemia, resulting in a high RBC count and low Mentzer index (<13). Target cells are a hallmark. Option A usually has a Mentzer index >13. Option C is macrocytic. Option D shows spherocytes, not target cells. Option E shows sickle cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a severe bacterial infection shows a total WBC count of 25,000/mm3. On the peripheral smear, the examiner notes the presence of band form neutrophils, toxic granulations, and Dohle bodies. What is this constellation of findings called?
questions.explanation: A left shift refers to the release of immature neutrophil precursors (like bands) into the peripheral blood, usually during infection. Toxic granulations and Dohle bodies are signs of activated neutrophils. Option A is a malignancy. Option B is an extreme left shift (WBC >50,000). Option D and E refer to decreased cell counts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 7-year-old boy presents with easy bruising and spontaneous nosebleeds. Laboratory tests show a normal Platelet count, normal PT, but a significantly prolonged aPTT. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) causes an isolated prolongation of aPTT (intrinsic pathway) with normal PT and platelet counts. Option A would show low platelets. Option B might show a slightly prolonged aPTT but usually also has abnormal platelet function tests. Option D and E would typically show both prolonged PT and aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a blood transfusion, a patient suddenly develops fever, chills, and lower back pain. Laboratory analysis shows hemoglobinuria and a positive direct Coombs test. What is the most likely mechanism of this reaction?
questions.explanation: This is an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR). Pre-formed recipient antibodies (usually anti-A or anti-B) attack the donor RBCs, leading to complement activation and intravascular hemolysis. This is a classic Type II hypersensitivity. Option A describes anaphylaxis. Option C describes serum sickness. Option D describes graft vs host disease. Option E describes febrile non-hemolytic reactions (caused by cytokines).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To differentiate between a Leukemoid Reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following laboratory findings would be most helpful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in a reactive leukemoid reaction (as the neutrophils are mature and functional) but low in CML (as the malignant cells lack the enzyme). Option A, B, and D can be present in both conditions. Option E is non-specific. Note: Modern practice uses BCR-ABL testing, but LAP score is the classic comparative test in pathology.

options.B: Presence of immature cells in peripheral smear

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the early stages of iron deficiency anemia, which of the following changes occurs first in the sequence of iron depletion?
questions.explanation: The sequence of iron deficiency is: 1. Depletion of stores (low ferritin, low marrow iron), 2. Erythropoiesis impairment (low serum iron, high TIBC), and 3. Anemia (low Hb, then microcytosis). Therefore, marrow depletion is the earliest change. Option A, B, and E are late findings. Option D is the second stage.

options.C: Depletion of bone marrow iron stores

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old patient has a blood smear showing large, hypersegmented neutrophils and macro-ovalocytes. You suspect B12 deficiency. Which of the following biochemical findings would confirm B12 deficiency over folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: Both B12 and folate deficiency cause increased homocysteine. However, only B12 is a cofactor for the conversion of methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA; thus, MMA only rises in B12 deficiency. Option A is not specific. Option B is for folate deficiency. Option D and E are related to other types of anemia (iron deficiency and hemolytic).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a history of excessive bleeding is suspected of having a platelet disorder. Platelet aggregation studies show no aggregation with the addition of Ristocetin, but normal aggregation with ADP and Collagen. The addition of normal plasma corrects the Ristocetin defect. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Ristocetin induces platelet aggregation by facilitating the binding of vWF to GP Ib receptors. In vWD, the lack of vWF prevents this. Adding normal plasma provides vWF, correcting the defect. Option A involves a GP IIb/IIIa defect (abnormal aggregation with everything except Ristocetin). Option B involves a GP Ib defect; however, the defect would NOT be corrected by normal plasma because the receptor on the platelet is missing. Option D is a clotting factor deficiency. Option E affects cyclooxygenase.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy of a patient with refractory anemia shows erythroid hyperplasia with many ringed sideroblasts (iron-laden mitochondria encircling the nucleus of erythroid precursors). This finding is most characteristic of a defect in which of the following?
questions.explanation: Sideroblastic anemia results from a defect in heme synthesis (e.g., protoporphyrin synthesis). Iron enters the mitochondria but cannot be incorporated into heme, leading to the ringed sideroblast appearance. Option A is for megaloblastic anemia. Option C is for Thalassemia. Option D is for iron deficiency. Option E is related to pure red cell aplasia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification of her anemia?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV less than 80 fL. Iron deficiency is the most common cause. 1. Normocytic anemia involves an MCV of 80-100 fL. 2. Macrocytic anemia involves an MCV greater than 100 fL. 3. Macrocytic hypochromic is an extremely rare or invalid combination as larger cells usually contain more hemoglobin. 4. Normocytic hypochromic is usually an early stage of microcytic anemia but not the primary classification for an MCV of 72.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the peripheral blood film of a patient suspected of having Megaloblastic Anemia, which of the following white blood cell findings is considered pathognomonic?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (5 or more lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia due to impaired DNA synthesis affecting all myeloid lineages. 1. Toxic granulations occur in severe bacterial infections. 2. Pelger-Huet cells are seen in myelodysplastic syndromes. 3. Left shift indicates immature forms in acute inflammation. 4. Dohle bodies are inclusions seen in infections or burns.

options.D: Neutrophilic leukocytosis with a left shift

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old male presents with occult blood in his stool. His blood report shows hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and low serum ferritin. What is the most likely cause of his anemia?
questions.explanation: In an elderly male, iron deficiency anemia (low ferritin) is caused by chronic gastrointestinal blood loss until proven otherwise. 1. B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia with high MCV. 2. Hemolysis usually presents with jaundice and high reticulocyte count. 3. Folate deficiency is seen in malnutrition and causes macrocytosis. 4. Bone marrow aplasia causes pancytopenia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a severe bacterial infection shows a total leukocyte count of 25,000/mm3. This reactive increase in the white blood cell count is termed as:
questions.explanation: Leukocytosis is the increase in total white blood cells above the reference range, often due to infection. 1. Leukemia is a malignant proliferation of WBCs. 2. Leukopenia is a decrease in WBC count. 3. Aplastic anemia refers to the failure of all blood cell lines. 4. Polycythemia is an increase in red cell mass.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: According to the basic concept of blood grouping, an individual with Blood Group O negative is considered the universal donor because their red cells lack:
questions.explanation: Group O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh (D) antigens, preventing immune attack by the recipient's antibodies. 1. Lacking only A would still leave B antigens. 2. Lacking only B would still leave A antigens. 3. Lacking only Rh would still leave AB antigens. 4. Both A and Rh missing still leaves B antigens.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with a suspected bleeding disorder involving primary hemostasis, which first-line laboratory investigation would be most useful?
questions.explanation: Primary hemostasis involves platelets and the vessel wall; thus, platelet count and bleeding time are the first-line tests. 1. PT evaluates the extrinsic pathway. 2. aPTT evaluates the intrinsic pathway. 3. Thrombin time evaluates fibrinogen conversion. 4. D-dimer is used to detect fibrinolysis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is characterized by a reactive eosinophilia?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a hallmark of parasitic infections and allergic reactions. 1. Viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. 2. Iron deficiency doesn't typically affect the eosinophil count. 3. B12 deficiency leads to macrocytic anemia and neutropenia. 4. Bacterial pneumonia causes neutrophilia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: The term "Pancytopenia" in a laboratory report refers to:
questions.explanation: Pancytopenia is the simultaneous reduction of all three cellular components of blood (RBCs, WBCs, and Platelets). 1. Increase in all lines is seen in polycythemia vera. 2. Increased WBCs is leukocytosis. 3. Decreased RBCs is anemia. 4. Increased platelets is thrombocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with Rheumatoid Arthritis has a Hemoglobin of 10 g/dL. Her serum iron is low, but her serum ferritin is high. What is the primary mechanism of her anemia?
questions.explanation: Anemia of chronic disease is mediated by Hepcidin, which inhibits ferroportin, trapping iron in macrophages and preventing its use. 1. High ferritin rules out dietary lack. 2. DNA synthesis failure is seen in megaloblastic anemia. 3. Spleen destruction is hemolytic anemia. 4. Joints do not lose blood externally to cause systemic iron deficiency.

options.B: Sequestration of iron in macrophages by Hepcidin
options.D: Increased destruction of RBCs in the spleen
options.E: Chronic blood loss from the joints

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is treated with Filgrastim (recombinant G-CSF) to recover from chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. Which of the following is the most common adverse effect associated with this hematopoietic growth factor?
questions.explanation: G-CSF causes rapid expansion of the marrow, which often manifests as significant bone pain. 1. Red man syndrome is associated with vancomycin. 2. DVT is more common with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents. 3. Iron overload is a complication of repeated transfusions. 4. Acute renal failure is not a common side effect of G-CSF.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following sets of laboratory findings is most consistent with the diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency is microcytic (Low MCV). Stores are depleted (Low Ferritin), and the body's capacity to bind iron increases (High TIBC). 1. High ferritin occurs in inflammatory states. 2. High MCV is macrocytic. 3. Normal MCV is normocytic. 4. TIBC is usually high when iron stores are low.

options.A: Low MCV, High Ferritin, Low TIBC
options.B: Low MCV, Low Ferritin, High TIBC
options.C: High MCV, Low Ferritin, High TIBC
options.D: Normal MCV, High Ferritin, High TIBC
options.E: Low MCV, Normal Ferritin, Low TIBC

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old male presents with petechiae and mucosal bleeding. His platelet count is 15,000/mm3, while PT and aPTT are within normal limits. Which classification of bleeding disorder does this belong to?
questions.explanation: Isolated thrombocytopenia (low platelets) with normal coagulation times (PT/aPTT) characterizes a platelet-related bleeding disorder. 1. Factor deficiencies prolong PT or aPTT. 2. Fibrinolytic defects usually cause oozing from sites but don't drop platelets in isolation. 3. Vessel wall abnormalities (like Scurvy) present with normal labs. 4. Combined defects would show abnormal PT/aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: How can Folate deficiency be distinguished from Vitamin B12 deficiency on clinical and laboratory grounds?
questions.explanation: B12 deficiency leads to subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord due to defective methylation; folate deficiency lacks these neurological features. 1. Both cause macrocytic anemia. 2. Both show hypersegmented neutrophils. 3. Both show elevated homocysteine. 4. Folate stores are small (months), while B12 stores are large (years).

options.A: Folate deficiency causes macrocytic anemia, while B12 does not
options.B: B12 deficiency is associated with neurological symptoms, while folate is not
options.C: Hypersegmented neutrophils are only seen in B12 deficiency
options.D: Serum homocysteine is elevated only in B12 deficiency
options.E: Folate deficiency takes years to develop, while B12 takes weeks

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction caused by ABO incompatibility, what is the primary immunologic mechanism of red cell destruction?
questions.explanation: ABO antibodies (IgM) are potent complement activators, causing rapid, life-threatening intravascular hemolysis. 1. ADCC is a slower process. 2. Delayed hypersensitivity takes 24-48 hours. 3. Sequestration in the spleen (extravascular) is more common with Rh incompatibility. 4. Antibodies do not primarily act by inhibiting intracellular enzymes.

options.D: Sequestration of cells in the lymph nodes
options.E: Inhibition of red cell enzymes by antibodies

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a bone marrow aspiration of a patient with Megaloblastic Anemia, the characteristic finding of "nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony" refers to:
questions.explanation: In megaloblastic anemia, DNA synthesis is impaired, so the nucleus stays immature (large, open chromatin) while RNA synthesis and hemoglobin (cytoplasm) continue normally. 1. Nuclei do not mature faster; they are delayed. 2. Both do not fail; the cytoplasm is relatively advanced. 3. Premature release is not the definition of asynchrony. 4. Iron in mitochondria refers to sideroblastic anemia.

options.A: Nuclei maturing faster than the cytoplasm
options.B: Cytoplasm maturing faster than the nucleus
options.C: Nucleus and cytoplasm both failing to mature
options.D: Premature release of cells into peripheral blood
options.E: Excessive iron accumulation in the mitochondria

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear shows many "Target cells" and "Howell-Jolly bodies." Which underlying mechanism best explains these findings in a patient with chronic anemia?
questions.explanation: The spleen normally removes Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants) and excess membrane; in its absence, target cells and HJ bodies persist. 1. Iron deficiency shows pencil cells. 2. Lead poisoning shows basophilic stippling. 3. B12 shows hypersegmentation. 4. Acute blood loss shows normocytic cells and reticulocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following malignant disorders of white blood cells is categorized by the presence of the Philadelphia chromosome [t(9;22)]?
questions.explanation: CML is defined by the BCR-ABL fusion gene on the Philadelphia chromosome. 1. ALL can sometimes have it, but it is not the defining hallmark as in CML. 2. CLL is associated with 13q deletions. 3. Hodgkin involves Reed-Sternberg cells. 4. Multiple myeloma involves plasma cell clones and M-proteins.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemia, a high Red Cell Distribution Width (RDW) is most characteristic of:
questions.explanation: RDW measures anisocytosis (variation in size). Iron deficiency begins gradually, leading to a mixed population of small and normal cells, thus a high RDW. 1. Thalassemia minor usually has a uniform population (Low MCV, normal RDW). 2. Anemia of chronic disease is usually uniform. 3. Spherocytosis has uniform small cells. 4. Aplastic anemia cells are uniform.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following indices represents the average volume of a single red blood cell?
questions.explanation: Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) measures the average size/volume of erythrocytes. Distractor A (MCH) measures the average weight of hemoglobin per cell. Distractor B (MCHC) is the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. Distractor D (RDW) measures the variation in RBC size (anisocytosis). Distractor E (Hematocrit) is the ratio of the volume of red blood cells to the total volume of blood.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old woman presents with pallor and fatigue. Lab results show a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and an MCV of 72 fL. What is the most common cause of this morphological type of anemia?
questions.explanation: An MCV below 80 fL indicates microcytic anemia, for which iron deficiency is the most common cause globally. Distractors A and B cause macrocytic anemia (MCV > 100 fL). Distractors D and E typically present as normocytic anemia (MCV 80-100 fL).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily produced in the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is the primary hormone stimulating RBC production, secreted by the kidneys. Distractor A (Thrombopoietin) is mainly produced in the liver to stimulate platelet production. Distractors C, D, and E are produced by various cells (like macrophages, T-cells, or fibroblasts) and stimulate different lineages or multipotent cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the peripheral blood film of a patient with suspected Megaloblastic Anemia, which white blood cell finding is highly characteristic?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (5 or more nuclear lobes) are a classic hallmark of megaloblastic anemia due to impaired DNA synthesis. Distractor A is seen in severe bacterial infections. Distractor C is characteristic of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML). Distractor D is diagnostic for Hodgkin Lymphoma. Distractor E is a benign nuclear shape variation or a myelodysplastic feature.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which blood group is considered the universal donor for packed red blood cells because it lacks both A and B antigens?
questions.explanation: Group O negative red cells lack A, B, and Rh antigens, making them safe for emergency transfusion to most recipients. Distractors A, B, and E contain antigens (A, B, or Rh) that can cause reactions in sensitized or mismatched recipients. Distractor C is the universal recipient for cells but the universal donor for plasma.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12-year-old boy has spontaneous joint bleeds (hemarthrosis). Lab tests show a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) and a normal Prothrombin Time (PT). Which factor deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Prolonged aPTT with normal PT and hemarthrosis suggests Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency). Distractor A (Factor VII) would prolong PT, not aPTT. Distractors C, D, and E are part of the common pathway and would prolong both PT and aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with a reactive increase in neutrophils (Neutrophilia)?
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections trigger a neutrophilic response as part of the innate immune system. Distractor A typically causes lymphocytosis. Distractors C and D are classic causes of eosinophilia. Distractor E does not typically alter the WBC count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory test is specifically used to monitor the extrinsic and common pathways of coagulation, often used for patients on Warfarin?
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic pathway (Factor VII) and common pathway. Distractor A assess platelet function. Distractor B monitors the intrinsic pathway (Heparin monitoring). Distractor D assesses the final step of fibrinogen to fibrin conversion. Distractor E is an obsolete test for platelet function.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Tiny, pin-point purple spots on the skin caused by a very low platelet count are known as:
questions.explanation: Petechiae are 1-2 mm hemorrhages associated with thrombocytopenia. Distractor A refers to larger bruises (>1 cm). Distractor B refers to medium-sized hemorrhages (3-10 mm). Distractor D is a localized collection of blood in tissues. Distractor E is simply redness of the skin due to inflammation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is used clinically to reduce the duration of neutropenia in patients receiving chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: G-CSF stimulates the production of granulocytes (neutrophils). Distractor A is used for anemia. Distractor C is used for thrombocytopenia. Distractor D is for clotting factor synthesis. Distractor E is a supplement for iron deficiency.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old strict vegan presents with megaloblastic anemia and numbness/tingling in his feet. What finding differentiates this from folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: Only Vitamin B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (neurological symptoms) due to defective myelination. Distractors A, C, D, and E can be seen in both B12 and Folate deficiencies as they both cause megaloblastic changes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In Anemia of Chronic Disease, what is the primary mechanism of low serum iron despite adequate bone marrow iron stores?
